issue_title: Implement fail-slow for the local engines
issue_body:
This is a demonstration of the solution to #915 which can be done easily now.
Each engine keeps a mapping of promise to product returned by the plan executor.  Just need to trap raise from the executor and if fail slow, associate a poision product with the promise.  Then when calling plan.bind going forward in following plan executions, we can check if the binding has grabbed any poisoned products and if so, short circuit that particular execution.

issue_title: go_remote_libraries does not document its packages param
issue_body:
On https://www.pantsbuild.org/build_dictionary.html go_remote_libraries does not have any documentation for its `packages` attribute.

As far as I can tell, https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/1d5f565f9c7c89e14b76a8a43f611915fb98c45e/src/python/pants/help/build_dictionary_info_extracter.py#L195-L200 grabs the thing for which a target is considered an alias, and uses its args. https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/1d5f565f9c7c89e14b76a8a43f611915fb98c45e/contrib/go/src/python/pants/contrib/go/register.py#L33 registers go_remote_libraries as an alias of go_remote_library using a macro wrapper, which the build_dictionary_info_extracter doesn't know how to see through.

I'm not sure how best to fix this. I tried a few quick things, but none of them worked. I suspect the answer may just be to not define go_remote_libraries as a macro, and instead define it as a proper target.

@jsirois this seems like your corner of the world...

issue_title: After #535, validating PolyModelType fields copies them (potentially dropping some fields)
issue_body:
Let's look on the `PolyModelType._convert` in case it is called when validating an already converted instance.

Before the fix for the recursive validation, the `self.is_allowed_model(value)` check skipped constructing a new model object when validating (although, it skipped validation, too, which was the issue in the first place) and simply returned the object itself. After #535, this check is not applied when validating an existing instance, so the `PolyModelType._convert` function proceeds to determine a suitable `model_class` unnecessarily, and then construct a new instance of this class from the existing one, instead of validating in-place. I think that this behavior is quite unexpected.

Then, the current semantics of doing `m.__class__(m)` for a model instance seem to not be well-defined. I would expect the instance to be copied (either shallow, or deep). But currently the existing instance gets re-interpreted as a raw data dictionary, and the full convert/validate process is performed. What makes it worse, when converting the model to dict, simple iteration over the model instance is done.
This is the `Model.__iter__` implementation:
```
    def __iter__(self):
        return (k for k in self._schema.fields if k in self._data
            and getattr(self._schema.fields[k], 'fset', None) is None)
```
It seems to intentionally drop any fields with a setter. I fail to understand why this is needed, but it results in any serializable fields with a setter being dropped on copy. 
Which, in turn, leads to dropping some serializable-with-a-setter fields inside a `PolyModelType` field during an **in-place validation** of the top-level instance, which seems pretty bad.

The following test passes with schematics-2.0.1 and fails with schematics-2.1.0:
```
import uuid

import schematics
from schematics import types as st, exceptions as se


class TestModel(schematics.Model):

    _raw_input = None

    @st.serializable(st.UUIDType(serialize_when_none=False, serialized_name='input'))
    def input_dyn(self):
        return self._raw_input

    @input_dyn.setter
    def input_dyn(self, value):
        self._raw_input = value


class TestModelContainer(schematics.Model):
    inner: TestModel = st.PolyModelType(TestModel, required=True)


def test_serializable_in_polymorphic():
    raw_input = '90fccdbf-5a1d-4cc6-a0ae-5537a8754b79'
    native_input = uuid.UUID(raw_input)

    model2 = TestModelContainer({'inner': {'input': raw_input}},
                                validate=True, apply_defaults=False, partial=False).inner
    model2.validate()  # Related to issue https://github.com/schematics/schematics/pull/535?
    assert isinstance(model2, TestModel)
    assert model2.input_dyn == native_input
```

issue_title: Icon Request: fa-fabric/industry
Remove "null" from fields and entities
issue_body:
The base `Field` class has the following signature:

```python
    def __init__(
            self,
            required=False,
            choices=None,
            default=_SENTINEL,
            null=False):
```

The "required", "choices" and "default" attributes are all used when generating random data for testing purposes. However, the "null" attribute is not used at all, anywhere, to the best of my knowledge. This is silly. Why should a completely useless attribute be bumming around?

Drop the `null` attribute. Doing so will not affect anything right now, given that there is no usage of the field. It should be done before version 1.0 is released, as dropping the attribute is a backward-incomptable change. (Some client may reference the attribute.) In addition, the attribute can be added again after version 1.0 is released without breaking anything - only prompting a minor version number bump.

issue_title: If a task kills a worker, an error should be pushed to the relevant driver.
issue_body:
Consider the following task which kills a driver.

```python
import ray

ray.init(redirect_output=True)

@ray.remote
def f():
  eval("exit()")

ray.get(f.remote())
```

This script will hang and no error message will be pushed to the driver. At the very least, an error message should be pushed to the driver. We may also want to prevent the driver from hanging. This can be done in two ways.

1. Figure out the object IDs that were supposed to be created by the task and put an object in the object store.
2. Figure out the object IDs that were supposed to be created by the task and put a value in Redis indicating that those objects failed. Then in ray.get, periodically check Redis to see if that value is present.

issue_title: Move plasma object store into Arrow.
issue_body:
The object store is a standalone component that can be used independently of Ray. There is some interest from the Apache Arrow community in merging the object store into Arrow so that it can be used by a wider audience of Arrow users.

Progress is being made on preparing the object store for merging into Arrow in #579.

**Note:** We currently have two separate processes, the **object store** and the **object manager**. The **object store** is a simple key-value store (there is one on each machine). Clients of the plasma store can memory map files allocated by the store into their own address spaces, write objects, read objects, and things like that. The **object manager** is a Ray specific process that interacts with Redis to determine which machines contain which objects and things like that. It itself is a client of the local object store (there is also one object manager per machine), and it initiates and handles transfers of objects between object stores. It's possible that some components of the object manager make sense in Arrow as well, but in its current form, the manager is very Ray specific.

issue_title: in2csv fails to load codepage 21010 xls files
issue_body:
This is a common issue in CSV parsers/Excel exporters apparently: 

- https://github.com/tafia/calamine/pull/86 "Treat codepage 21010 as codepage 1200"
- https://github.com/SheetJS/js-xls/issues/44 "xls: error parsing bad.xls: Error: Unrecognized CP: 21010"
- https://github.com/PHPOffice/PHPExcel/issues/396 "Unknown codepage: 21010"

It seems to be generated from MacOS versions of Excel. 

Basically, when encountering codepage 21010 it should interpret it as codepage 1200 (AKA UTF-16le).

Ideally this would be handled programmatically. However, even passing in `--encoding utf-16le` (or other variations) seem to have no effect on `in2csv`, so it might be ignoring the encoding argument?

issue_title: Problem with oscar.core.loading._import_module() in debug mode in Eclipse + PyDev
issue_body:
I'm porting a Oscar v0.8 project across to v1.0 from scratch and have hit a problem when running the debugger in Eclipse and PyDev that seems to stem from `oscar.core.loading._import_module()`.

I am extending the catalogue app and when run from the normal Eclipse Run it goes fine but when run using Eclipse Debug I get

```
ImportError: No module named signals
```

I have not extended `myshop.catalogue.signals` (I've only added some fields to `models.py`).

The problem comes about in `oscar.core.loading._import_module()`

```
__, __, exc_traceback = sys.exc_info()
frames = traceback.extract_tb(exc_traceback)
if len(frames) > 1:
    raise
```

with PyDev running in debug mode `frames` contains:

```
[('/Users/<user>/.virtualenvs/myshop/lib/python2.7/site-packages/oscar/core/loading.py', 159, '_import_module', 'return __import__(module_label, fromlist=classnames)'), ('/Applications/eclipse-kepler/plugins/org.python.pydev_3.9.0.201411111611/pysrc/_pydev_imps/_pydev_pluginbase.py', 445, 'plugin_import', 'fromlist, level)')]
```

so it triggers the second condition detailed in the comments for `_import_module()`.

The quick fix is to set 

```
if len(frames) > 2:
```

and to just be aware that import errors may occur from inside the overridden module.  
I'm not sure if this is only peculiar to my development setup, but I just wanted to flag it here in case someone else was experiencing this issue.

issue_title: Partly using API in an oscar website
issue_body:
In a scenario where an already existing website tries to use some part of the API (eg. implement ajax cart), basket managment is broken.
oscarapi uses session to manage basket_id whereas oscar uses cookies.
If the basket addition is done using API and a redirect happens for checkout or any other functionalty, request.basket is empty.
I solved this problem by overwriting basket APIs to use request.basket instead of operations.get_basket.
But, then the clients not using cookies wont be able to use the api.
So, had to override oscar.apps.basket.middleware to use session to manage basket.id.

Question is "Is having such a hybrid approach a bad idea altogether? If not then shoudn't we have a common approach to managing basket?"

issue_title: Use stacklevel in warnings
issue_body:
When you do ``warn('blah')`` somewhere in scikit-image, the user gets *that* line-number whenever the warning is raised.  If the warning message is "you've used a deprecated keyword argument", e.g., the correct line to return is *where the user called skimage*.  To fix this, use the *correct* stacklevel argument in ``warn``.

For an example of correct usage, take a look at numpy.

issue_title: Image inversion of uint64

## Description
There is a new problem with image inversion. As far as I can tell, it is not related to issue #3028 nor to PR #3030, as it concerns unsigned integer inversion. It is pretty enigmatic to me ... here it is:
```
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: from skimage.util import invert

In [3]: imin = np.array([0, 1], np.uint64)

In [4]: imout = invert(imin)

/Users/twalter/pycharm/scikit-image/skimage/util/_invert.pyc in invert(image, signed_float)
     63         inverted = ~image
     64     elif np.issubdtype(image.dtype, np.unsignedinteger):
---> 65         max_val = dtype_limits(image, clip_negative=False)[1]

```

issue_title: No links created for preprocessor macros
issue_body:
*[Copied from [BitBucket](https://bitbucket.org/birkenfeld/sphinx-contrib/issues/80/doxylink-no-links-created-for-preprocessor)]*

Preprocessor macros documented using Doxygen can not be linked using doxylink. For instance, this code:

```cpp
/*! \file */
/// A simple macro
#define MACRO(x) foo(x)
```

will generate an entry in the Doxygen tag file but the tag entry will not be available for use in reStructuredText documentation:

```rest
The :project:`MACRO` is simple.
```

does not link to the documentation.

The problem appears to be in doxylink.py at line 148:

```python
if arglist_text and member_kind != 'variable' and member_kind != 'typedef' and member_kind != 'enumeration':
```

Changing this line to:

```python
if arglist_text and member_kind != 'variable' and member_kind != 'typedef' and member_kind != 'enumeration' and member_kind != 'define':
```

gets things working for me.

I have an issue with the save_m2m method when using UUID as the resource ID. I get duplicate related records created even though the records already exist (as indicated by the fact they have resource_uris).

```javascript
	]
}
```

This creates 2 more industries and adds them to industries relation rather than just adding the relation to the existing industry. 

I suspect this is another hard coding of PK instead of field specified in detail_uri_name.

Models:
```python

	uuid 					= models.UUIDField(default=uuid.uuid4)
	name 					= models.CharField(max_length=255)
```

issue_title: Current version of ElasticSearch do not support '_status'
issue_body:
`_status` was deprecated from v1.2 and removed in v2.0. [Current documentation](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/indices-status.html), recommends using `_stats` instead. Due to this, `ElasticSearch.status()` will raise the following exception: `ElasticHttpError: (400, u'illegal_argument_exception')`.

I have never used `_status` before, so I'm not sure of the data that is sent back, but I would assume that using `_stats` would be sufficient as a replacement

issue_title: Max force error in drone defuses children for amorphous structures
issue_body:
There's some error analysis code in the drone that checks for max_forces and if its over a certain amount it sets the calculation state to error. This is especially problematic for amorphous structures which are often optimized very loosely. As a result a lot of amorphous optimizations "error" at the drone state and defuse children.

The max_force_threshold in the optimization firework should somehow propagate to the drone, and behave in the same way across custodian and the drone, e.g. None means ignore this.

issue_title: @validates_schema(pass_original=True) doesn't get "original value"
issue_body:
Sorry for the length of the ticket, however I hope these tests demonstrate what I'm talking about. With `2.13.5` and:

    class Foo(Schema):
        count = 0
        class Meta:
            strict = True
        field = fields.Str()

        @validates_schema()
        def validate_extra(self, data):
            Foo.count += 1

First one:

```python
def test_1():
    req = Mock()
    req.mimetype = 'application/json'

    req.get_json = lambda *_, **__: {}
    FlaskParser().parse(Foo(), req=req, locations=('json', ))  # called
    assert Foo.count == 1

    req.get_json = lambda *_, **__: []
    FlaskParser().parse(Foo(), req=req, locations=('json', ))  # called
    assert Foo.count == 2

    req.get_json = lambda *_, **__: [{}]
    FlaskParser().parse(Foo(), req=req, locations=('json', ))  # called
    assert Foo.count == 3
```

So `@validates_schema` is always called here. Nice.

If we add `many=True`:

```
def test_2():
    req = Mock()
    req.mimetype = 'application/json'

    req.get_json = lambda *_, **__: {}
    FlaskParser().parse(Foo(many=True), req=req, locations=('json', ))  # not called
    assert Foo.count == 0

    req.get_json = lambda *_, **__: []
    FlaskParser().parse(Foo(many=True), req=req, locations=('json', ))  # not called
    assert Foo.count == 0

    req.get_json = lambda *_, **__: [{}]
    FlaskParser().parse(Foo(many=True), req=req, locations=('json', ))  # called!
    assert Foo.count == 1
```

Why isn't `@validates_schema()` not called for `{}` and `[]`? I guess it's because we default to `pass_many=False`:

So add `pass_many=True`:

```python
def test_3():
    class Bar(Foo):
        @validates_schema(pass_many=True)
        def validate_extra(self, data, many):
            Foo.count += 1

    req = Mock()
    req.mimetype = 'application/json'

    req.get_json = lambda *_, **__: {}
    FlaskParser().parse(Bar(many=True), req=req, locations=('json', ))
    assert Foo.count == 1

    req.get_json = lambda *_, **__: []
    FlaskParser().parse(Bar(many=True), req=req, locations=('json', ))
    assert Foo.count == 2

    req.get_json = lambda *_, **__: [{}]
    FlaskParser().parse(Bar(many=True), req=req, locations=('json', ))
    assert Foo.count == 3
```

Now `@validates_schema` is called always. Good.

However... with `pass_original=True`, the `orig_data` gets passed to `@validates_schema` is empty list:

```
def test_4():
    class Baz(Foo):
        @validates_schema(pass_many=True, pass_original=True)
        def validate_extra(self, data, orig_data, many):
            raise Exception("got: " + str(orig_data))

    req = Mock()
    req.mimetype = 'application/json'

    req.get_json = lambda *_, **__: {'first': 1}
    with pytest.raises(Exception) as e:
        FlaskParser().parse(Baz(many=True), req=req, locations=('json', ))  # called!
    assert e.value.args[0] == "got: []"
```

`{'first': 1}` here gets passed in as [] (as do all non-list values, as empty list). Why is that?

Sorry if I should just RTFM, I just find this the behavior a bit confusing. Also, this is probably related to https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/616.

issue_title: problem with image.order=random in config
issue_body:
I think I found either (a) a problem with the implementation of image.order=random in config or (b) an undesirable feature of how the GREAT3-like example scripts ```cgc.yaml``` etc. were set up.

Here is the issue:
- ```cgc.yaml``` has ```image.order=random``` so that the 90-degree rotated pairs aren't right next to each other.
- The output images indeed don't have 90 degree pairs next to each other.
- The output truth tables DO list the 90 degree pairs right next to each other, and there's no information that one could use to figure out where they actually live in the image.

So I'm not sure if this is a bug in ```image.order=random``` implementation, where the locations aren't getting propagated into the truth catalog part of the code, or if it's just that the truth catalogs should have another output column in this example that tells you where to find the galaxies.  But something weird is definitely going on.

issue_title: graph_models doesn't handle unicode verbose_names correctly
issue_body:
When using the `-n` flag in `graph_models` to use the `verbose_name`s of the models and fields, it doesn't handle unicode strings properly when they contain _actual_ non-ascii characters.

## How to reproduce the error

    mkdir test_extensions
    cd test_extensions/
    mkvirtualenv test_extensions
    pip install django django_extensions pygraphviz
    django-admin startproject test_extensions .
    ./manage.py startapp test_app

Now add the following to `test_app/models.py`:

```python
class MyTestModel(models.Model):

    year = models.PositiveIntegerField(
        verbose_name= u'Año'
    )

    class Meta:
        verbose_name = u"MyTestModel with año"
        verbose_name_plural = u"MyTestModels with año"
```

Add `django_extensions` to `INSTALLED_APPS` and then try to run:

    ./manage.py graph_models -g -o myapp_models.png -R -S -n -a

This is the error that appears:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "./manage.py", line 10, in <module>

  File "/home/jose/.virtualenvs/test_extensions/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/core/management/__init__.py", line 338, in execute_from_command_line
    utility.execute()
  File "/home/jose/.virtualenvs/test_extensions/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/core/management/__init__.py", line 330, in execute
    self.fetch_command(subcommand).run_from_argv(self.argv)
  File "/home/jose/.virtualenvs/test_extensions/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/core/management/base.py", line 393, in run_from_argv
    self.execute(*args, **cmd_options)
  File "/home/jose/.virtualenvs/test_extensions/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/core/management/base.py", line 444, in execute
    output = self.handle(*args, **options)
  File "/home/jose/.virtualenvs/test_extensions/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django_extensions/management/utils.py", line 57, in inner
    ret = func(self, *args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/jose/.virtualenvs/test_extensions/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django_extensions/management/commands/graph_models.py", line 77, in handle
    dotdata = generate_dot(args, cli_options=cli_options, **options)
  File "/home/jose/.virtualenvs/test_extensions/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django_extensions/management/modelviz.py", line 141, in generate_dot
    model['label'] = appmodel._meta.verbose_name.decode("utf8")
```

issue_title: PubSub: Batching / Duplicates issue when consuming messages sequentially.
issue_body:
## Env Info 
python 3.5.3
google-cloud-pubsub==0.32.0
macOS 10.13.3

## Problem
I’m currently dealing with a weird issue on PubSub. In the video in the link there are two subscribers that process messages from the same subscription sequentially with thread.Lock and flow_control = 1. The ack deadline of the topic is 10s. 

With a publisher I first send 300 messages (just the number i) to the topic in one batch. The subscribers print the message.

As you can see in the beginning only one of the subscribers starts to receive messages. After some number of messages the second one kicks in (it seems to be number of messages not time - I tried it with adding a sleep in processing the messages). But both subscribers are processing the same messages.

After I clear the buffer I sent the same number of messages again with a batch_size of only 20. Then the two subscribers act as expected. They start at the same time and don’t process duplicate messages.

I assume the problem has to do with how the python consumers pull and caches messages.

issue_body: **OS type and version** macOS 10.13.5 (alpine linux used in Production) Python 3.6.4 google-cloud-vision v0.31.1 This issue is specific to the quality of ocr output under a specific circumstance. If there is a more appropriate place to post this issue, please let me know. **Summary** The google cloud vision ocr document_text_detection output has a severe drop in quality for tabular data. In some circumstances, it appears to ocr the same text more than once (overlapping text) and in other circumstances misses text altogether. We've only noticed this happen in table cells containing 5 or fewer characters. The results are typically high quality otherwise. I posted a quick example highlighting the issue. Original document is black. GCV text detection overlain in red. **Example output.** Text in original document was rotated 90°. This document was rotated before sending to the api so that the text was un-rotated. Several blocks of text were missed. **Example output** Text in original document was rotated 90°. This document was **not** rotated before sending to the api, so the text was sent to the api rotated. Several blocks of text were ocr'd twice. (Several T4S cells have overlapping "T4S" and "TAS" text.

issue_title: Unexpected Behaviour renaming nested workflow as the step name
issue_body:
I am running the dummy [nested workflow example](http://www.commonwl.org/user_guide/22-nested-workflows/) from the user guide which is running the [1st workflow example](http://www.commonwl.org/user_guide/21-1st-workflow/) as nested workflow.

## Expected Behavior
The nested workflow should have be referred by its name in the logged info:
```
[workflow nested-workflow.cwl] starting step compile
[step compile] start
[workflow 1st-workflow.cwl] start
[workflow 1st-workflow.cwl] starting step untar
[step untar] start
```

## Actual Behavior
The outer step name is used as nested workflow name:
```
[workflow nested-workflow.cwl] starting step compile
[step compile] start
[workflow compile] start
[workflow compile] starting step untar
[step untar] start
```

## Workflow Code
```
#!/usr/bin/env cwl-runner

cwlVersion: v1.0
class: Workflow

inputs: []

outputs:
  classout:
    type: File
    outputSource: compile/classout

requirements:
  - class: SubworkflowFeatureRequirement

steps:
  compile:
    run: 1st-workflow.cwl
    in:
      inp:
        source: create-tar/tar
      ex:
        default: "Hello.java"
    out: [classout]

  create-tar:
    requirements:
      - class: InitialWorkDirRequirement
        listing:
          - entryname: Hello.java
            entry: |
              public class Hello {
                public static void main(String[] argv) {
                    System.out.println("Hello from Java");
                }
              }
    in: []
    out: [tar]
    run:
      class: CommandLineTool
      requirements:
        - class: ShellCommandRequirement
      arguments:
        - shellQuote: false
          valueFrom: |
            date
            tar cf hello.tar Hello.java
            date
      inputs: []
      outputs:
        tar:
          type: File
          outputBinding:
            glob: "hello.tar"

```
## Your Environment
* cwltool version: 1.0.20180403145700
